nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical coral reefs, reef flats, lagoons, and rocky substrates; often seen aggregated in crevices or open sand by day, more active at night.

decorPreferences: Mature live rock with established algae and biofilm growth
Crevices and overhangs for daytime shelter
Securely stacked or epoxied rockwork to prevent toppling

diet.notes: Has been documented as a prolific algae grazer in the wild, consuming filamentous and fleshy algae as well as biofilms on live rock. In captivity, generally advised to be supplemented with dried marine algae (nori) sheets and algae-based pellets, as urchins in algae-poor tanks have been reported to starve. Will sometimes consume coralline algae from rockwork.

avoidWith: Large wrasses that prey on echinoderms

summary: A striking black Indo-Pacific reef urchin with extraordinarily long, mildly venomous spines. Generally considered reef-safe and an effective algae grazer, but must be handled with caution and housed in established tanks with secure rockwork.
careNotes: VENOMOUS — the long, hollow, needle-like spines deliver a mildly venomous sting roughly comparable to a bee sting, and the brittle spines easily break off in flesh; gloves and care are generally advised during any tank maintenance, and special caution applies for keepers with allergies. The test (body) is small (around 5–7 cm across), but spines commonly project 10–15 cm and can reach 25 cm or more in large adults, so plan rockwork accordingly. Generally considered reef-safe (does not eat coral tissue) and is widely valued as an effective grazer of hair algae, films, and other nuisance algae on live rock. However, it has been reported to bulldoze loose rocks, dislodge frags, and topple poorly-secured aquascapes — particularly in smaller tanks — so rockwork should be epoxied or securely stacked. Sources widely agree on water parameters (around 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG ~1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12) but disagree on minimum tank size: LiveAquaria does not state a specific minimum, ReefApp cites 200 L, and many hobbyist guides cite 50 gal / ~190 L; 200 L has been chosen as a nice round value that accommodates spine span and grazing range. Should generally only be added to tanks that have matured for several months and have steady algae growth — newly-set-up systems have been reported to lead to starvation. Highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to abrupt salinity or temperature shifts; slow drip acclimation is generally advised. The Caribbean congener Diadema antillarum (Long-spined Sea Urchin) is sometimes sold under the same trade name and has effectively identical husbandry needs (same parameters, same venomous-spine warning, same algae-grazing role).

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source for temperature (72-78°F / 22-26°C), pH (8.1-8.4), specific gravity (1.023-1.025), dKH (8-12), reef-safe status, peaceful temperament, easy care level, herbivore diet, and venomous-spine warning (bee-sting equivalent). Tank size not specified on the page.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403 Forbidden; substituted with ReefApp Encyclopedia, Wikipedia, and The Aquarium Wiki as alternative secondaries.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked minimum tank size (200 L / 53 gal), reef-safe status, peaceful temperament, average care level, algae-grazing diet, and Indo-Pacific distribution. Documented the species' tendency to overturn loose corals/rocks.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy (Diadematidae / Diadematoida), test diameter (~70 mm / 7 cm), test height (~40 mm), mildly venomous hollow spines, Indo-Pacific distribution from Red Sea to Japan and Australia, prolific algal grazer.

additional.name: SeaLifeBase / animalia.bio aggregate
additional.notes: Cross-checked reef-associated habitat with depth range 0-70 m. Direct page fetch returned 403; data taken from indexed search snippets and Springer/Wikipedia aggregates. Lifespan estimates (3.5-6 years) drawn from Springer Nature population studies of Mediterranean invasive populations.
